objections.description: MCP tool calls are specified with incorrect/assumed parameter schemas. There's no validation that the tool names (`mcp__ui-copilot__recommend_ui_sources`, `mcp__ui-copilot__plan_ui_execution`, `mcp__ui-copilot__generate_ui_agent_prompt`) actually exist or that the parameter shapes (`goal`, `stack`, `needsAnimation`, etc.) match the real tool schemas. If these tools don't exist or have different signatures, the skill will fail silently or hallucinate tool calls. The skill needs a defensive check pattern: attempt the call, handle failure gracefully, and fall back to built-in knowledge. Right now Steps 3, 5, and 6 all say 'If MCP tools are available' but never specify HOW to detect availability — Claude Code will just attempt the call and error out.
objections.suggested_fix: Add an explicit preamble instruction: 'Before calling any MCP tool, check if it is available in the current session. If a tool call fails or the tool is not registered, silently fall back to built-in recommendations. Never surface MCP tool errors to the user.' Also add a note that the parameter schemas shown are illustrative and the skill should adapt to the actual tool schema if it differs. Consider making MCP integration a clearly marked optional enhancement section rather than weaving it into the core process flow.

objections.description: The skill instructs Claude to read `src/components/`, `tailwind.config.*`, and `package.json` but provides no fallback for when these don't exist. Many Vite+React projects use `app/` or `lib/` structures, monorepos with `packages/*/`, or have tailwind config in `postcss.config.js` with `@tailwindcss/postcss`. The skill also doesn't account for Tailwind v4 which uses CSS-based configuration (`@theme` directive in CSS files) rather than `tailwind.config.js`. This is a real-world gap that will cause the skill to skip context gathering entirely for modern setups.
objections.suggested_fix: Replace hardcoded paths with a search strategy: 'Look for Tailwind configuration in: `tailwind.config.{js,ts,mjs,cjs}`, `postcss.config.*`, or CSS files containing `@theme` (Tailwind v4). Look for components in: `src/components/`, `src/app/`, `app/`, `components/`, or any directory containing `.tsx` files with React component exports. Check `package.json` at project root or nearest `package.json` in a monorepo.' Add explicit Tailwind v4 awareness — CSS-based config with `@theme` blocks, `@variant dark`, and `@utility` directives.

objections.description: The z-index scale uses values z-60, z-70, z-80 which are NOT default Tailwind utilities. Tailwind ships with z-0 through z-50 by default. Using z-60/z-70/z-80 requires custom theme extension, but the skill presents them as if they work out of the box. A developer following this guidance will get no CSS output for those classes unless they extend their config.
objections.suggested_fix: Either: (1) Add explicit instruction to extend the Tailwind theme: `zIndex: { 60: '60', 70: '70', 80: '80' }` in tailwind.config, or (2) Use arbitrary values `z-[60]` `z-[70]` `z-[80]` in the recommendation, or (3) Restructure the scale to use default Tailwind values (z-10, z-20, z-30, z-40, z-50) and note that arbitrary values should be used for anything beyond z-50.

objections.description: The hero code uses semantic color classes like `bg-background`, `text-foreground`, `text-primary`, `text-primary-foreground`, `text-muted-foreground`, `bg-card`, `text-card-foreground`, `bg-accent`, `ring-border` — these are shadcn/ui CSS variable conventions, NOT default Tailwind. A vanilla Tailwind project will render none of these colors. The skill claims to 'default to raw Tailwind + React implementations that work with zero additional dependencies' but the code contradicts this by depending on a shadcn/ui-style color token system.
objections.suggested_fix: Either: (1) Provide truly vanilla Tailwind code examples using default palette (`bg-white dark:bg-gray-950`, `text-gray-900 dark:text-gray-50`, etc.), OR (2) Add a required setup section showing the CSS custom properties these classes depend on and the tailwind.config extension needed, OR (3) Present both variants — one for shadcn/ui projects (detected by `components.json`), one for vanilla Tailwind projects. Option 3 is cleanest. The code exemplars should match the 'zero additional dependencies' claim.

objections.description: The guidance says 'desktop-first for admin dashboards' but Tailwind CSS is inherently mobile-first — unprefixed utilities apply to all sizes, and `sm:`/`md:`/`lg:` apply at minimum widths. Writing desktop-first in Tailwind requires using `max-*` variants which are less ergonomic and non-standard. This advice will confuse developers who then try to write desktop-first Tailwind and find themselves fighting the framework.
objections.suggested_fix: Clarify the nuance: 'Tailwind is mobile-first by default. For admin dashboards, DESIGN desktop-first (start your design thinking at desktop) but IMPLEMENT mobile-first in Tailwind (write base styles for mobile, then add `md:`/`lg:` overrides for desktop). Alternatively, use `max-md:` variants for specific mobile overrides if the desktop layout is your natural base.' This distinction between design thinking and implementation approach matters.

objections.description: The Tailwind animation classes `animate-in fade-in slide-in-from-bottom-4` are from `tailwindcss-animate` plugin (commonly bundled with shadcn/ui), not from core Tailwind CSS. A vanilla Tailwind project won't have these classes. This is another instance of assuming shadcn/ui ecosystem without declaring the dependency.
objections.suggested_fix: Replace with actual vanilla Tailwind approach: define a `@keyframes` in your CSS and reference via `animate-[fadeInUp_300ms_ease-out]` arbitrary value, or provide the keyframe definition that should be added to `tailwind.config.js` theme extension. Alternatively, label the column as 'tailwindcss-animate plugin' and provide the vanilla CSS alternative alongside.

suggestions.description: The pattern reference table and decision trees cover happy-path UI but completely omit error states, empty states, loading states, and 404/error pages. These are among the most commonly neglected UI patterns and exactly where developers need gallery-quality inspiration. saaspo.com specifically curates empty state patterns. Add a section covering: empty states (first-use, no-results, error), loading skeletons, error boundaries with recovery actions, and toast/notification patterns.

Missing several high-impact anti-patterns: (1) Using `onClick` on `<div>` elements instead of `<button>` (partially covered but should be more explicit about event handlers), (2) Fixed-height containers with overflow:hidden that clip content at different font sizes, (3) Using `gap` without fallback on older Safari (increasingly less relevant but still in enterprise), (4) Nesting interactive elements (button inside a link), (5) Color as the ONLY indicator of state (fails for colorblind users). The existing anti-patterns are good but skew toward visual/performance concerns and underweight interaction/semantic issues.

Only two code exemplars are provided (hero and card). For a skill claiming to cover navigation, CTAs, pricing, forms, and animation, this is thin. Add at minimum: a sticky nav with mobile drawer, a CTA contrast band, and a scroll-reveal wrapper component. These are the patterns developers struggle with most and where concrete code has the highest ROI. They don't need to be exhaustive — 20-line focused examples are sufficient.

Principle 7 mentions Core Web Vitals targets (LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1, INP < 200ms) but nowhere in the skill is there guidance on how the recommended patterns affect these metrics. For example: hero images need `fetchpriority="high"` and `loading="eager"`, below-fold images need `loading="lazy"`, font loading strategy affects LCP. Add a brief performance checklist tied to the patterns you recommend — e.g., 'Hero images: use `` with fetchpriority="high", explicit dimensions, and preload the LCP image in ``.'

The scope check says 'For single-component requests, skip directly to Step 3' but Step 3 is the MCP tool call. It should say 'skip directly to Step 7 with a condensed Layout + Pattern + Code output' or reorganize so the code delivery step is correctly referenced. This is a process flow bug.

At ~4000+ words, this is pushing the practical limit for a Claude Code slash command file. While the content is high quality, Claude Code command files that are too large can crowd out context window space for actual code. Consider: (1) Moving the full pattern decision trees into a separate markdown file that gets `@`-referenced, (2) Keeping the command file focused on the process steps and MCP integration, (3) Using the separate reference file for the detailed pattern catalogs and anti-patterns. This would also make the pattern reference independently updatable.
